text: [Leontien de Waal](https://www.abnamro.nl/nl/zakelijk/insights/authors/leontien-de-waal/index.html)

metadata.source_title: From goat's wool socks to pure profit

text: Architect Paul de Ruiter won the ABN AMRO Sustainable 50 award and consultant Jip van Grinsven the Young Professional award. Their mission? Changing the image of sustainability: no nonsense, because smart building is simply good business. Paul designs buildings that use energy and materials more intelligently. Jip provides insight into the financial side of circularity. Based on their own expertise, they show that we should not see sustainability as an expensive obstacle, but as a valuable matter of course.

metadata.source_title: From goat's wool socks to pure profit

text: In 1992, Paul started doing his PhD on energy-efficient buildings. “After two years, my supervisor said: maybe you should start making them.” So he founded his architectural firm in 1994, fully focused on sustainability. 'I had read the Global 2000 Report, in which US President Carter predicted what climate change would cause. At that time, every architect was still concerned with the beauty of buildings. Important, but I didn't think that was enough: responsible construction makes a building even more beautiful.'

metadata.source_title: From goat's wool socks to pure profit

text: At that time, according to Paul, sustainability still had a goat-wool image. 'Small-scale, with wood and earth, somewhat clumsy-looking. I just wanted to show that it can also be done in a modern way. My philosophy: form follows climate – not only function and form, but also sustainability determines the design.'

metadata.source_title: From goat's wool socks to pure profit

text: > “In 1994, sustainability still had a goat-wool image. I wanted to show that it can also be done in a modern way.”

metadata.source_title: From goat's wool socks to pure profit

text: Jip did not immediately feel strongly about the subject. “During my training, sustainability always came last, it was never really part of it,” she says. It wasn't until she graduated that her perspective changed. 'I discovered that it actually offers economic opportunities.' At consultancy firm Alba Concepts she specialized in the financial side of circular construction. For example, she created a brochure in which she compares the circularity of various housing construction concepts. 'Around 75 providers are already participating in this. I also look at the financial valuation of circularity. Not everyone builds sustainably from conviction. But if it makes financial sense, why wouldn't you do it?'

metadata.source_title: From goat's wool socks to pure profit

text: > “Not everyone builds sustainably out of conviction. But if it makes financial sense, why wouldn't you do it?”

metadata.source_title: From goat's wool socks to pure profit

text: Their work shows that sustainability is a wise choice. A good example is the building that Paul designed for the Erasmus University Rotterdam: 8,500 square meters for three thousand students. 'Ventilation is an energy guzzler in such a building. We have developed a system that works with solar and wind power. The result: 80 to 85 percent energy savings, fewer installations, more fresh air.'

metadata.source_title: From goat's wool socks to pure profit

text: Jip's innovation lies in his accounting. 'We usually completely depreciate buildings over forty years. But with circular construction you are left with products that still have value. So you do not depreciate to zero euros, but to a certain residual value. This will reduce your annual costs.'

metadata.source_title: From goat's wool socks to pure profit

text: > “Installations have a significant CO2 footprint and must be replaced every fifteen years. Smart design saves money, maintenance and replacement.”

metadata.source_title: From goat's wool socks to pure profit

text: Both are annoyed by the misunderstanding that sustainability is slow and expensive. “For example, you can save a significant amount of concrete by building smarter, which saves time and money,” says Paul. 'In addition, installations have a significant CO2 footprint and must be replaced every fifteen years. If you design smartly, fewer are needed. And that saves money, maintenance and replacement.'

metadata.source_title: From goat's wool socks to pure profit

text: Failure costs are also a problem. 'In construction that is more than five percent. By industrializing and automating, you can significantly reduce it,” Paul explains. Jip: 'Concept construction is also faster. So when politicians say: sustainability has to give way because we have to accelerate housing construction, that is simply not true. Sustainable methods are precisely the solution.'

metadata.source_title: From goat's wool socks to pure profit

text: > “Valuation only looks back, but we should look forward. Not only accountants have to take circularity into account, appraisers too.”

metadata.source_title: From goat's wool socks to pure profit

text: For Paul, health is the next point of attention. 'Biobased materials are often non-toxic. This affects absenteeism and productivity. However, attention is not yet being paid to this everywhere. Strict occupational health and safety requirements apply to offices, but no amount of daylight or ventilation has been determined for classrooms. While there are thirty children and a teacher there. As far as I am concerned, the well-being of building users will be higher on the agenda.'

metadata.source_title: From goat's wool socks to pure profit

text: Jip focuses on appraisers. 'Valuation only looks back, but we should look forward. Not only accountants have to take circularity into account, appraisers too. Circularity requires a future-oriented view. That is why we are setting up a new sustainable valuation and real estate agency at Alba Concepts in January 2026: Waerdig. Purely to show that it is possible.' According to both, sustainability should become self-evident. “Just like “the economy,”' says Paul. “You cannot be for or against the economy, it is just there.”

metadata.source_title: From goat's wool socks to pure profit

text: The ABN AMRO Sustainable 50 is the list of professionals who demonstrably contribute to a more sustainable built environment. They are the drivers, connectors, accelerators and innovators in the sector. Every year we put these people on stage to honor their work, commitment and vision and to inspire others to get moving. There are awards in three categories: maker, inventor and doer. The winners were announced on November 5, 2025 during the Climate Summit for the Built Environment.

metadata.source_title: From goat's wool socks to pure profit

text: The Dutch construction sector has been one of the best performing sectors in recent years, despite several challenges facing the sector. Examples are the nitrogen dossier, the significant price increases for energy and building materials and also the personnel shortages. Driven by trends, innovations and legislation and regulations, the relevance of themes such as sustainability and environmental impact is growing and the sector is looking for a new balance.
